result:
I keep thinking about the moment just before a story starts—those few invisible seconds when the page is still blank, the cursor still blinking, and every possible version of the next paragraph exists at once. It’s the closest thing I know to standing at the edge of a diving board at night: you can’t see the water below, only feel the board trembling under your weight with every heartbeat. Most of the time we hurry off that plank, eager to be already mid-air, already committed. We write the first sentence just to escape the vertigo of *not* having written it.

But lately I’ve been lingering. I’ve been letting the cursor blink until my screen dims and the room around me turns into a fish tank of blue light. I’ve been trying to listen to the hush that precedes the first word, because I suspect something alive hides in there—something that gets flattened the instant language pins it down. It’s like the half-second before you name a feeling: the emotion is vast, liquid, multicolored, and then the moment you say *grief* or *joy* it shrinks into a single opaque marble you can roll around in your pocket. Useful, portable, but no longer oceanic.

Maybe that’s why I still write by hand when I can. The pen’s ink is a slow umbilical cord; it lets the thing on the other side stay wet and writhing a little longer. The page absorbs the hesitation, the micro-tremor in the wrist that wonders whether *fracture* might be more honest than *break*. A keyboard delivers the alphabet like a deck of punched cards; a pen delivers it like a trail of blood you can follow back to the body if you ever need proof you were alive.

I used to think the goal was to keep fracturing forward—sentence after sentence—until you arrived at some final shore where meaning waited, fully dressed. Now I think the real magic is in the fracture itself: the hairline crack where the light of what you meant leaks into what you actually said, and the two refuse to reconcile. That crack is haunted. Walk past it at night and you hear the murmur of every draft you deleted, every love letter you rewrote until it sounded *reasonable*. They are still down there, glowing like subway mice with eyes full of reflection.

Some evenings I open old notebooks and discover marginalia I don’t remember authoring: a phone number with no name, a pencil sketch of a key that opens no door I still possess. Artifacts from an archaeological layer of self I can’t carbon-date. I touch the paper and feel the same vertigo as when I stare at the cursor: the sense that another me is standing at the other end of the notebook, counting on me to finish the sentence so he can keep existing. If I stop writing, do we both vanish?

Outside my window the city performs its nightly trick of turning strangers into constellations. Each lit window is a syllable in a story I’ll never read. Someone practices saxophone scales; someone else lifts a cat off a countertop; a third person Googles “how to forgive yourself for something you did on purpose.” The neighborhood is a vast collective rough draft, and the silence between our actions is the blank space where the cursor of the world keeps blinking.

I think I write to keep that cursor awake. To keep the possible from collapsing into the merely actual. To stay suspended above the water a fraction of a second longer, where every belly-flop and every perfect dive still share the same unbroken horizon.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
